value.prior_session_context: June 25 followed Wednesday's mixed close (S&P 500 7,358.22, Nasdaq 25,476.64) and was dominated by two scheduled catalysts: the 8:30 a.m. EDT release of the May PCE inflation report and the market's reaction to Micron's after-the-bell fiscal Q3 results from June 24. The S&P 500 finished essentially unchanged and the Nasdaq Composite fell for a fourth consecutive session.

text: Thursday, June 25, 2026 was a regular trading day for US cash-equity and futures markets, with no market holidays. The session was framed by the 8:30 a.m. EDT release of the May Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) price index and by the equity reaction to Micron Technology's fiscal third-quarter results, reported after the close on June 24. US stocks drifted to a mixed close: the S&P 500 ended essentially flat, the Dow rose, and the Nasdaq Composite fell for a fourth straight day.

sp_500.basis: AP: down 0.73 points (less than 0.1%); TheStreet close update -0.01%; previous close 7,358.22

dow.basis: AP: up 71.72 points (+0.1%); TheStreet close update +0.14%; previous close 51,848.90

nasdaq_composite.basis: AP: down 118.03 points (-0.5%); TheStreet close update -0.46%; previous close 25,476.64

russell_2000.basis: AP: up 21.23 points (+0.7%) to 3,007.86 from 2,986.63. AP is the sole authoritative close source for the Russell 2000; TheStreet's intraday +0.38% box figure is inconsistent with the +0.71% implied by AP's point change and is not used.

calculation.method: AP: S&P 500 at 7,357.49 down 0.73 points from 7,358.22; Dow at 51,920.62 up 71.72 points from 51,848.90; Nasdaq at 25,358.60 down 118.03 points from 25,476.64; Russell 2000 at 3,007.86 up 21.23 points from 2,986.63. TheStreet's 4:01 p.m. EDT close update independently lists S&P 500 7,357.49 (-0.01%), Nasdaq 25,358.60 (-0.46%), and Dow 51,920.62 (+0.14%).

text: US equity indexes closed mixed on Thursday, June 25, 2026. The S&P 500 slipped 0.73 points (less than 0.1%, TheStreet -0.01%) to 7,357.49, the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 71.72 points (+0.1%) to 51,920.62, the Nasdaq Composite fell 118.03 points (-0.5%) to 25,358.60 in its fourth straight decline, and the Russell 2000 rose 21.23 points (+0.7%) to 3,007.86. Week-to-date, the S&P 500 is down 1.9%, the Dow is up 0.7%, the Nasdaq is down 4.4%, and the Russell 2000 is up 0.9%. Year-to-date, the S&P 500 is up 7.5%, the Dow is up 8.0%, the Nasdaq is up 9.1%, and the Russell 2000 is up 21.2%.

headline.note: Highest annual headline rate since April 2023 (TheStreet); up from 3.8% YoY in April 2026

core_ex_food_energy.note: Core highest since October 2023 (TheStreet); up from 3.3% YoY in April 2026

vs_consensus.core: both monthly and annual in line with forecasts

market_session: Pre-market macro release, 8:30 a.m. EDT Thursday 2026-06-25
text: The Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge, the May PCE price index, was released by the Bureau of Economic Analysis at 8:30 a.m. EDT. The headline index rose 0.4% on the month and 4.1% from a year earlier — the highest annual rate since April 2023 and well above the Fed's 2% target. Core PCE, excluding food and energy, rose 0.3% on the month and 3.4% year-over-year, the highest since October 2023. The annual headline matched the Dow Jones consensus while the monthly figure came in 0.1 percentage point below expectations; both core readings were in line. Personal income rose 0.7%, real PCE rose 0.3%, and the personal saving rate was 3.0%. The data was read as inflation behaving 'pretty much as economists expected,' which let Treasury yields ease.

value.revenue_basis: LSEG consensus; revenue more than quadrupled from $9.3B a year earlier
value.gross_margin_context: up from 74.9% the prior quarter and 39% a year earlier; Investopedia: 'nearly 85% from under 40%'
value.stock_move_basis: Investopedia close section 'jumped 15%'; CNBC headline 'jumps 15%' (extended trading after June 24 print); TheStreet midday +15.78%. Intraday quote widget showed +17.31% and is excluded.
value.trailing_context: Micron stock up roughly 700% over the past year; market cap past $1 trillion

market_session: US regular cash-equity close, Thursday 2026-06-25 (reaction to 2026-06-24 after-bell results)
text: Micron Technology was the single biggest positive driver. Its fiscal Q3 results, reported after the bell on June 24, beat sharply: revenue of $41.46 billion (vs $35.84B LSEG consensus) more than quadrupled from $9.3 billion a year earlier, adjusted EPS was $25.11 (vs $20.78), and gross margin jumped to 84.9% from 39% a year ago on a memory shortage tied to AI data-center demand. Micron guided fiscal Q4 revenue to about $50 billion (vs $43.58B expected) and declared a 15-cent July dividend. The stock rose about 15% on the session, reviving a memory and chip rally that had lost steam earlier in the week. Micron's stock is up roughly 700% over the past year, carrying its market cap past $1 trillion.

apple.driver: Cut on news Apple hiked prices on Macs and iPads/MacBooks; Tim Cook had told the WSJ that 'unsustainable' memory costs could make price hikes 'unavoidable'; Investopedia notes the drop nearly wiped out Apple's year-to-date gain (trading near $277)

microsoft.driver: Announced Xbox console price increases amid the memory crunch (512GB +$100 effective Aug. 1)

nvidia.basis: Investopedia 3:38 p.m. EDT: gave up early gains to trade 2% lower (intraday, mutable)

value.theme: Magnificent 7 / mega-cap tech retreat outweighed the Micron-led memory rally and capped the S&P 500 and Nasdaq

text: Mega-cap technology was the dominant drag and the reason the S&P 500 and Nasdaq could not hold gains. Apple fell about 6% (TheStreet close -6.13%) after it raised prices on Macs and iPads, days after CEO Tim Cook warned the Wall Street Journal that 'unsustainable' memory costs could make price hikes 'unavoidable'; the slide nearly erased Apple's year-to-date gain. Microsoft fell 3.23% after announcing Xbox console price increases tied to the same memory crunch. Nvidia gave up early gains to trade about 2% lower and Broadcom slipped about 1% on Investopedia's late-afternoon read (intraday, mutable). The Magnificent 7's retreat outweighed the Micron-led memory rally.

value.interpretation: Micron's blowout results 'breathed life into a memory and chip stock rally that lost steam earlier this week,' lifting peer memory names even as broad mega-cap tech fell

market_session: US regular cash-equity session (intraday late-afternoon), Thursday 2026-06-25
text: Micron's results revived the memory and chip-stock rally that had stalled earlier in the week. Micron rose about 15%, fellow memory maker Sandisk was up about 22%, and Western Digital gained about 5% on Investopedia's late-afternoon read. These peer moves are intraday-stamped (3:38 p.m. EDT) and mutable; they are carried at medium confidence to distinguish them from the AP-anchored index closes. The rally was sector-specific: memory names rose on Micron's demand signal while the broader large-cap technology complex fell.

ten_year.note: Investopedia: 10-year dropped to 4.40% from 4.42% seen before Thursday's inflation data. TheStreet: fell to 4.394% by the US market close, its lowest point since mid-May.

value.driver: Yields eased after the May PCE report showed inflation behaving roughly as economists expected

text: Treasury yields eased after the in-line PCE report. The 10-year Treasury yield slipped to about 4.40% from 4.42% just before the inflation data (Investopedia), and TheStreet put it at 4.394% by the US market close — its lowest level since mid-May. AP attributed the move to a report showing inflation behaving 'pretty much as economists expected.'

wti_crude.basis: Investopedia 3:38 p.m. EDT: rose more than 2% to $72 a barrel (intraday, mutable; no settlement figure sourced)

brent_crude.basis: Investopedia 3:38 p.m. EDT: up a similar amount at $75.50 a barrel (intraday, mutable)

value.catalyst: Reports that Iran (IRGC) struck a cargo/Singaporean vessel transiting the Strait of Hormuz amid an apparent standstill in US-Iran negotiations; a reversal of the prior several sessions' oil decline on peace hopes
value.context: Strait traffic had picked up since the US and Iran agreed to cease hostilities

text: Oil prices reversed higher after several sessions of declines. West Texas Intermediate rose more than 2% to about $72 a barrel and Brent crude gained a similar amount to about $75.50, on reports that Iran's Revolutionary Guard struck a cargo vessel transiting the Strait of Hormuz amid an apparent standstill in US-Iran negotiations. These are intraday-stamped levels (Investopedia 3:38 p.m. EDT, TheStreet 2:11 p.m. EDT) with no official settlement figure sourced for this cycle; they are mutable and carried at medium confidence with explicit as_of.

dow_leaders.sectors: strength in health care, financials, and industrials offset mega-cap tech losses and lifted the price-weighted Dow

small_caps.note: AP-anchored close; small caps had the strongest showing, closing just above 3,000

value.interpretation: Like Wednesday, the session bifurcated: the cap-weighted, tech-heavy indexes were dragged by mega-cap tech while the Dow and small caps gained on broader participation

text: Beneath the flat headline indexes, breadth was positive. The price-weighted Dow was lifted by Caterpillar (+5.81%) and UnitedHealth Group (+2.65%) and by strength in health care, financials, and industrials, which offset the mega-cap tech losses. Small caps did best: the Russell 2000 rose 0.7% to 3,007.86 (AP close), closing just above 3,000. The pattern echoed Wednesday's bifurcation, with the cap-weighted, tech-heavy indexes held back by a handful of mega-cap names while the rest of the market advanced.

gold.basis: Investopedia 3:38 p.m. EDT: gold futures rose ~1% to $4,045 after dipping below $4,000 on June 24 for the first time since November (intraday, mutable)

bitcoin.note: Near its lowest level this year; earlier fell to ~$58,000 (lows last seen in 2024), off more than 50% from an October peak of $126,000 (intraday, mutable)

fed_commentary.speaker: Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee
fed_commentary.quote: core inflation 'too high' and 'trending the wrong way'
fed_commentary.policy_context: A majority of Fed leaders favor raising rates later this year; the Fed signaled a willingness to hike last week

market_session: US session intraday and post-close commentary, Thursday 2026-06-25
text: Across other assets, gold futures rose about 1% to $4,045 an ounce, recovering after dipping below $4,000 on June 24 for the first time since November. Bitcoin traded around $59,500, near its lowest level this year after briefly touching ~$58,000 (lows last seen in 2024) and off more than 50% from its October peak of $126,000. The US dollar index eased 0.2% to 101.45, and the Cboe Volatility Index rose about 3% to above 19. On policy, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee called core inflation 'too high' and 'trending the wrong way,' consistent with a majority of Fed officials favoring a rate hike later this year. Gold, Bitcoin, and dollar levels are intraday-stamped (3:38 p.m. EDT) and mutable; this claim is carried at medium confidence.

value.cross_market_context: normal regular session; European equities rallied to a record STOXX 600 close, led by healthcare with an AI-driven tech bounce that faded into the close, after the prior two-session pullback

text: European equity markets traded a normal regular session on Thursday, June 25, 2026, and closed broadly higher, with the pan-European STOXX 600 setting a record close. Live closing prints are evidenced by the LSEG FTSE 100 daily table (close as of 16:35 BST on volume of about 970 million shares), dated June 25 closes on the Trading Economics Germany and France pages, the Reuters European-close wrap, and the Il Sole 24 Ore close report. Daily moves are measured against the Wednesday, June 24 close. There is no cross-market calendar asymmetry: both European and US venues traded a normal session.

stoxx_europe_600.basis: Reuters cash close 640.21 / +0.8% (record close, intraday record 642.09); Trading Economics CFD EU600 638.86 / +0.58% (Jun/25); Reuters morning read about 638.11 / +0.46% by 0828 GMT; anchored to the Reuters cash close, TE-CFD divergence disclosed

calculation.method: STOXX 600: 640.21 minus 635.16 equals +5.05 (about +0.8%, Reuters cash close); EURO STOXX 50: 6,273.00 minus 6,219.46 equals +53.54 (+0.86%); DAX: 24,944.30 minus 24,740.36 equals +203.94 (+0.82%); CAC 40: 8,448.70 minus 8,385.49 equals +63.21 (+0.75%); FTSE 100: 10,529.89 minus 10,461.63 equals +68.26 (+0.65%); continental percentages are Trading Economics dated-table day-changes, the STOXX 600 is anchored to the Reuters cash close versus the Trading Economics CFD EU600

text: European equities closed broadly higher on Thursday, June 25, 2026. The pan-European STOXX Europe 600 rose about 0.8% to a record close of 640.21 (Reuters cash close, intraday record 642.09), while the Trading Economics CFD EU600 read 638.86 / +0.58% - a disclosed cash-close versus CFD divergence. The EURO STOXX 50 gained 0.86% to 6,273.00, Germany's DAX added 0.82% to 24,944.30 (from 24,740.36), and France's CAC 40 rose 0.75% to 8,448.70 on the stats-box close with a later live CFD at 8,426.70 / +0.49%. The UK's FTSE 100 added 0.65% to a precise 10,529.89. Daily moves are measured against the June 24 close.

market_session: London regular session close, Thursday 2026-06-25 (16:35 BST)

calculation.method: close 10,529.89 minus prior close 10,461.63 equals +68.26 points; +68.26 / 10,461.63 equals +0.65 percent

text: The FTSE 100 is the cycle's one precise official close and a solid gainer: on the Investors Chronicle/LSEG historical daily table it opened 10,461.52, traded a 10,415.45 to 10,575.31 range, and closed 10,529.89 on volume of 970,238,545 shares, up 68.26 points (+0.65%) from Wednesday's 10,461.63. London advanced with the broader European rally and set a fresh intraday high of 10,575.31.

subject.name: AI rally reignited by Micron and Qualcomm, a Bayer-led healthcare surge, and an oil reversal

value.primary_support: strong Micron and Qualcomm forecasts reignited the global AI rally and lifted European chip and tech names, while a Bayer surge drove healthcare and easing-then-reversing oil framed the macro backdrop
triggers: Micron and Qualcomm earnings and forecasts after the Wednesday US close
US Supreme Court reining in Bayer Roundup lawsuits
oil reversing from intraday lows to gains on a Strait of Hormuz fees report
US inflation above 4% in May keeping Fed and ECB hikes in play
value.tech: European tech (up 32% for the quarter) jumped as much as 2.5% intraday before fading to +0.8% at the close
value.rates: traders priced Fed and ECB rate hikes by year-end per LSEG data; 10-year Treasury yield up about 1 bp, German 10-year flat
value.oil: Brent fell about 1.2% to 72.8 dollars intraday then reversed to about +1.5% (74.8 dollars) by the European close

text: European shares rallied to a record STOXX 600 close on Thursday, led by healthcare and a revived AI trade. Strong forecasts from Micron and Qualcomm after the US close - Micron citing 22 billion dollars of committed memory-chip orders and Qualcomm 15 billion dollars of data-centre sales by 2029 - reignited the global AI rally, lifting Asian and European chipmakers; Europe's tech sector rose as much as 2.5% before fading to +0.8% at the close. Healthcare was the standout as Bayer jumped 18.7% to top the STOXX 600 after the US Supreme Court reined in Roundup cancer lawsuits. Oil swung from an intraday decline (Brent about -1.2% as tankers exited the Strait of Hormuz) to a gain after a report Tehran planned to charge fees to reopen the strait. US inflation broke above 4% in May, keeping Fed and ECB hikes priced by year-end.

leaders.healthcare_note: biggest boost to the STOXX 600; Bayer +18.7% on the US Supreme Court Roundup ruling
leaders.technology_note: closed +0.8% after gaining as much as 2.5% intraday; STOXX 600 tech sector index +0.9% per Il Sole; chipmakers led

laggards.aerospace_and_defence_note: defence at the bottom of the tape - Avio -6.62%, Leonardo -3.97%, Fincantieri -2.92% in Milan, Rheinmetall -1.78% in Frankfurt, Thales -2.71% at the Paris open
laggards.banks_note: Euro Stoxx Banks roughly flat at +0.09%; BNP Paribas +0.83%
laggards.automobiles_note: Stellantis -0.95% on weak European vehicle registrations, while Volkswagen rose on the Everllence sale

text: The tape rotated into healthcare and technology and out of defence. Per Reuters, healthcare was among the biggest boosts to the STOXX 600, up 1.5%, on Bayer's 18.7% jump, while the tech sector closed up 0.8% after gaining as much as 2.5% intraday (Il Sole put the STOXX 600 tech sector index at +0.9%). Defence was the clear laggard, with Milan's Avio -6.62%, Leonardo -3.97% and Fincantieri -2.92%, Frankfurt's Rheinmetall -1.78% and Paris's Thales -2.71% at the open. Banks were roughly flat (Euro Stoxx Banks +0.09%) and Stellantis fell 0.95% on weak European registrations. Sector percentages are carried at medium confidence; index-level closes are high confidence.

gainers.Bayer_note: topped the STOXX 600 on the US Supreme Court Roundup ruling
gainers.Infineon_basis: Reuters cash close +3.1%; Trading Economics component +5.46%; Reuters morning +5.6%
gainers.Volkswagen_note: agreed to sell Everllence diesel-engine unit to Bain Capital for about 7.4 billion euros (8.4 billion dollars)

decliners.SAP_basis: Trading Economics component -1.04% versus the page biggest-laggards note -2.1%

text: The day's standout was Bayer, up 18.7% to top the STOXX 600 after the US Supreme Court reined in Roundup cancer lawsuits. Chipmakers led the AI bounce - at the Reuters cash close Infineon +3.1%, STMicroelectronics +5.1%, ASML +2.6% and Siemens Energy +2.3%, with the Trading Economics CFD components higher (Infineon +5.46%) on a disclosed basis. Other gainers: 3i Group +11.4% on Action's sales, EasyJet +6.4% after rejecting a fourth Castlelake bid, Volkswagen about +1.3% to +1.5% on selling Everllence to Bain Capital for about 7.4 billion euros, plus Milan's STMicroelectronics +2.75%, Ferrari +1.58% and Enel +2%. Decliners were led by defence - Avio -6.62%, Leonardo -3.97%, Fincantieri -2.92%, Rheinmetall -1.78% - alongside Fresenius Medical Care -4.3%, Deutsche Boerse -2.1%, SAP (-1.04% to -2.1% by basis) and Stellantis -0.95%. Single-stock percentages are carried at medium confidence with cash-close versus CFD basis disclosed.

oil.note: declined as tankers exited the Strait of Hormuz, then reversed on a report Tehran would charge fees to reopen it

rates.us_10y: +1 bp after a 9 bp drop the prior day
rates.german_10y: flat, down 11 bps on the week
rates.pricing: Fed and ECB hikes priced by year-end per LSEG data

inflation.us_may: above 4% for the first time in three years

fx.euro: about 1.1367 to 1.138 dollars, near a 13-month low

gold.note: slid below 4,000 dollars for the first time in 2026 (about 3,995) then recovered to about 4,028

value.france_consumer_confidence: rose to 84 in June from 82, above the 83 expected

text: The cross-asset backdrop was constructive but two-sided. Oil fell intraday (Brent about -1.2% to 72.8 dollars) as stranded tankers exited the Strait of Hormuz, then reversed to a gain (Brent about +1.5% to 74.8 dollars) after a report Tehran planned to charge fees to reopen the strait. Easing rate pressure rallied bonds: the US 10-year yield was up about 1 bp after a 9 bp drop, and the German 10-year was flat (down 11 bps on the week). US inflation broke above 4% in May for the first time in three years while PCE came in line, keeping Fed and ECB hikes priced by year-end. The euro sat near a 13-month low around 1.137 to 1.138 dollars with the dollar near a one-year high, gold slid below 4,000 dollars for the first time in 2026 before recovering toward 4,028, and France's consumer confidence rose to 84 in June, above expectations.

value.prior_session_context: June 25 followed a mixed Asia session on June 24 (Hang Seng +0.3% at 23,412.18 snapping a five-day losing streak; KOSPI +3.26% at 8,471.02; Nikkei 225 -0.58% at 69,384 per TE news stream). The June 25 session was dominated by the after-the-bell Micron fiscal Q3 earnings beat from June 24 US trading, which catalyzed a region-wide chip-stock rally. Japan, South Korea, and mainland China benchmarks rose; Hong Kong and Australia fell.

market_session: Asia regular cash-equity sessions, Thursday 2026-06-25 (all closed)
text: Thursday, June 25, 2026 was a regular trading day for all referenced Asian cash-equity venues (Tokyo, Hong Kong, Shanghai, Shenzhen, Seoul, ASX), with no market holidays. The session was framed by the after-the-bell Micron Technology fiscal Q3 results from June 24 US trading, which beat sharply and catalyzed a region-wide chip-stock and AI-infrastructure rally. Japan, South Korea, and mainland China benchmarks rose sharply, while Hong Kong and Australia fell.

calculation.method: Nikkei 225 official close 72,366.34 vs prior 69,174.97; KOSPI 8,930.30 vs prior 8,471.02; Hang Seng 23,076.91 vs prior 23,412.18; Shanghai Composite 4,120.28 (Xinhua close); ASX 200 8,749.90 vs prior 8,808.40 (Trading Economics CFD close).

text: Asian equity indexes closed sharply mixed on Thursday, June 25, 2026. Japan's Nikkei 225 surged 3,191.37 points (+4.61%) to 72,366.34 and South Korea's KOSPI jumped 459.28 points (+5.42%) to 8,930.30, both led by chip-stock rallies on Micron's blowout earnings. Mainland China's Shanghai Composite added 0.23% to 4,120.28. Hong Kong's Hang Seng was the regional outlier, falling 335.27 points (-1.43%) to 23,076.91 — its lowest close since May 2025. Australia's ASX 200 slipped 58.50 points (-0.66%) to 8,749.90, a two-week low, as hawkish RBA commentary and weak gold miners weighed.

sk_hynix.basis: Trading Economics KOSPI news stream: 'SK hynix advanced sharply by ... 13.91%'; Reuters: 'closed higher at 13%'

samsung_electronics.basis: Trading Economics KOSPI news stream: 'Samsung Electronics ... 5.29%'

advantest.basis: Trading Economics JP225 component table Jun/25: 35,900 / +15.06%; news stream: 'Advantest (14.9%)'

tokyo_electron.basis: Trading Economics JP225 component table Jun/25: 75,340 / +7.78%; news stream: 'Tokyo Electron (8.1%)'

kioxia_holdings.basis: Trading Economics JP225 news stream: 'Kioxia Holdings (13.1%)'

taiyo_yuden.basis: Trading Economics JP225 news stream: 'Taiyo Yuden (11.3%)'

murata_manufacturing.basis: Trading Economics JP225 news stream: 'Murata Manufacturing (7.9%)'

value.catalyst: Micron Technology fiscal Q3 revenue $41.46B (vs $35.84B est), guidance ~$50B (vs $43.58B est); Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra: 'tight conditions to persist beyond calendar 2027'; $22B customer pre-pay commitments
value.interpretation: The chip-heavy Korean and Japanese benchmarks outperformed the region; the KOSPI +5.42% and Nikkei +4.61% were directly attributed by Trading Economics and Reuters to Micron-driven AI/memory demand optimism. SK hynix was additionally supported by its announced ~$29.4B (KRW 45.5T) Nasdaq ADR listing plan.

text: Asian chip and memory stocks surged on Micron's blowout earnings. South Korea's SK hynix closed +13.91% and Samsung Electronics +5.29%, lifted both by the Micron demand signal and by SK hynix's announced plan to raise up to KRW 45.5 trillion (~$29.4 billion) through a Nasdaq ADR listing. In Tokyo, the semiconductor test-equipment maker Advantest closed +15.06%, Tokyo Electron +7.78%, and unlisted-then-IPO'd Kioxia Holdings +13.1%, with Taiyo Yuden +11.3% and Murata Manufacturing +7.9%. J.P. Morgan recommended 'add on any dips' and maintained an overweight on South Korea; Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra said tight memory conditions should persist beyond calendar 2027. The chip rally was the single dominant force driving the KOSPI and Nikkei to their biggest one-day gains in the cycle.

hang_seng.basis: Yahoo Finance and Investing.com historical Jun/25; Trading Economics HK50 news stream: 'fell 335 points, or 1.4%, to close at 23,077'

context.southbound_inflows: Strong southbound inflows from mainland China supported Hong Kong equities amid IPO revival

losers.basis: Trading Economics HK50 component table Jun/25 and news stream

text: Hong Kong's Hang Seng was the regional outlier, falling 335.27 points (-1.43%) to 23,076.91 — its lowest close since May 2025 and a fresh 52-week-low intraday at 22,978.59. The slide came despite a chip-led regional risk-on tone, as investors remained cautious ahead of Hong Kong's trade balance data and as mega-cap internet and consumer names dragged: Tencent -1.73%, Meituan -2.44%, Xiaomi -2.87%, Sunny Optical Technology -11.7%, and Trip.com -10.9%. Strong southbound inflows from mainland China partly tempered losses. The divergence between the chip-driven Korea/Japan rallies and the Hong Kong underperformance was the session's defining cross-market structure.

shanghai_composite.basis: Xinhua: 'up 0.23 percent to 4,120.28 points'; Trading Economics SHANGHAI table Jun/25

chinent.basis: Xinhua: 'gained 2.84 percent to close at 4,371.99 points'

combined_turnover.basis: Xinhua: 'combined turnover of stocks covered by these two indices reached 3.59 trillion yuan (about 526.32 billion U.S. dollars), up from 3.28 trillion yuan on the previous trading day'

value.sector_leaders: Shares related to memory chips and ultracapacitors led the gains; the precious metal sector saw major declines (Xinhua)

text: Mainland Chinese benchmarks closed higher on Thursday. The Shanghai Composite added 0.23% to 4,120.28, the Shenzhen Component jumped 1.82% to 16,344.08, the ChiNext gained 2.84% to 4,371.99, and the STAR Composite rose 2.1% to 2,391.95. The CSI 300 closed at 5,020.10, up 1.56%. Combined turnover on the Shanghai and Shenzhen indices reached 3.59 trillion yuan (~$526.32 billion), up from 3.28 trillion yuan the prior session. Memory-chip and ultracapacitor shares led, while precious-metals stocks declined — the same memory-vs-gold pattern seen across the region.

usd_jpy.basis: Reuters global markets wrapup: 'The Japanese yen was near its lowest in 40 years against the dollar ... The yen was flat against the greenback at 161.80 per dollar.'

boj_rate.basis: Trading Economics Japan related indicators: Japan Interest Rate 1.00, previous 0.75, percent, reference Jun 2026

boj_commentary.speaker: BoJ board member Naoki Tamura
boj_commentary.stance: Hawkish — called for rate hikes every few months (Trading Economics news stream: 'BoJ Hawk Tamura Calls for Rate Hike Every Few Months')

market_session: Asia session Thursday 2026-06-25 (post-close FX quote from NY wrap)
text: The yen traded at 161.80 per dollar, flat on the day but near its lowest level in 40 years, with more intervention widely expected from Tokyo after the last bout around May did not stem the currency's decline. The Bank of Japan had raised its policy rate to 1.00% (from 0.75%), and BoJ board member Naoki Tamura — identified by Trading Economics as a hawk — called for rate hikes every few months. Japan's headline CPI was 1.50% year-over-year in May, up from 1.40% in April. The weak-yen backdrop supports the translation value of Japanese exporters' overseas earnings and was a tailwind for the Nikkei's chip-led rally, even as it raises intervention risk.

asx_200.basis: Trading Economics ASX200 CFD close Jun/25: 8,749.9 / -58.5 / -0.66%. News stream: 'slipped 60 points, or 0.7%, to close at 8,749'

macro_driver.employment: Stronger-than-expected rise in Australia's May employment
macro_driver.rba_commentary: Deputy Governor Andrew Hauser: inflation remains 'too high' and more work may be needed

losers.basis: Trading Economics ASX200 component table Jun/25 and news stream

text: Australia's ASX 200 slipped 58.50 points (-0.66%) to 8,749.90, a two-week low, as a stronger-than-expected May employment print reinforced expectations that the RBA will hold its hawkish stance, with Deputy Governor Andrew Hauser stressing that inflation remains 'too high.' Gold miners slid as bullion hit a seven-month low: Evolution Mining -3.9% and Northern Star Resources -3.1%. Energy names fell as oil retreated to pre-Iran-war levels: Woodside -3.3%, Santos -2.6%. Judo Capital plunged 39% after cutting its 2026 earnings outlook. The big four banks shed between 1.2% and 3.7%. Losses were partly capped by firmer US stock futures overnight.

brent_crude.basis: Reuters: 'Brent crude futures settled up 2% at $75.26 a barrel.' Context: pre-war levels as tankers pass through Hormuz

gold.basis: Reuters: 'Spot gold rose 0.64% to $4,026.09 an ounce.' Note: Asia-session gold miners sold off on bullion hitting a seven-month low (TE Australia wrap)

euro.basis: Reuters: 'The euro was last at $1.137, a whisker above Wednesday's 13-month low'

us_10y_yield.basis: Reuters: 'The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes fell 0.59 basis point to 4.394%'

value.interpretation: Easing oil prices amid US-Iran peace-talk progress and reopening Strait of Hormuz shipping were a key macro tailwind for oil-import-dependent Asia (especially Japan), helping risk appetite even as they weighed on Australian energy names. The weaker dollar and lower yields provided a supportive backdrop for regional equities, but gold's softness hit Australian gold miners.

market_session: NY close cross-asset reference for Asia session Thursday 2026-06-25
text: The cross-asset backdrop for the Asia session was supportive of risk appetite. Brent crude settled up 2% at $75.26 a barrel but remained near pre-US-Iran-war levels as tankers resumed passage through the Strait of Hormuz and peace-talk progress was signaled. The dollar index eased 0.15% to 101.45; the euro was at $1.137; the yen was flat at 161.80 but near 40-year lows. Spot gold rose 0.64% to $4,026.09 an ounce in NY trading, though Asian-session gold miners sold off on the weaker bullion tone. The US 10-year yield slipped to 4.394%. For oil-import-dependent Japan, the combination of a weaker yen (boosting exporter translations) and soft oil (easing import-cost pressure) was a double tailwind — even as the weak yen kept intervention risk on the radar.

value.primary_release: BEA May 2026 Personal Income and Outlays released June 25, 2026
value.dominant_macro_mix: elevated PCE inflation, resilient income and real spending, modestly lower Treasury yields, softer DXY, and oil still far below its prior-month level despite a daily rebound

text: June 25, 2026 was a normal Thursday macro session centered on the BEA May Personal Income and Outlays release. The official Treasury curve has a 06/25/2026 row, and the cross-asset tape combined elevated but not accelerating PCE inflation, resilient income and real spending, modestly lower Treasury yields, a slightly softer DXY, and Brent still far below its prior-month level despite a daily rebound.

BEA's May 2026 Personal Income and Outlays release shows personal income up $181.6B / 0.7%, DPI up $164.9B / 0.7%, current-dollar PCE up $156.1B / 0.7%, and real PCE up $43.8B / 0.3%. The PCE price index rose 0.4% month over month and 4.1% year over year; excluding food and energy, it rose 0.3% month over month and 3.4% year over year. The release also reports personal outlays up $159.9B, personal saving at $704.2B, and a 3.0% saving rate.

value.vendor_context: Trading Economics described the 10Y near 4.40% as the lowest in seven weeks and said it was down 0.10 percentage points over the past month.

calculation.method: read official Treasury par-yield CSV; 10Y minus 2Y; day change is 06/25/2026 row minus 06/24/2026 row, converted to basis points

text: The official U.S. Treasury par curve closed June 25 at 4.09% on the 2-year, 4.15% on the 5-year, 4.40% on the 10-year, 4.87% on the 20-year and 4.86% on the 30-year. Versus June 24, the 2-year and 5-year each fell 2 bp, the 10-year fell 1 bp, and the 20-year and 30-year were unchanged; 2s10s widened from +30 bp to +31 bp. Trading Economics separately described the 10-year near 4.40% as the lowest in seven weeks and down 0.10 percentage points over the past month.

value.commentary: Trading Economics said the dollar weakened slightly while investors assessed US data, lower oil prices, and Fed-policy implications.

calculation.method: vendor page reported DXY level and percentage changes; mutable Trading Economics basis disclosed in source metadata

text: On Trading Economics' mutable vendor basis, DXY was 101.4879 on June 25, down 0.12% from the previous session, up 2.34% over the past month and up 4.47% over the prior 12 months. The page commentary said the dollar weakened slightly while investors assessed the US data batch, lower oil prices, and Fed-policy implications.

value.basis_note: Trading Economics says displayed Brent prices are based on OTC/CFD instruments and do not represent official Brent benchmarks.
value.commentary: Trading Economics said traders monitored Strait of Hormuz shipments after a cargo ship was struck off Oman; the page also framed Brent as still near pre-war levels after progress in US-Iran peace efforts improved supply expectations.

calculation.method: vendor page reported Brent level and percentage changes on a CFD reference basis; mutable vendor basis disclosed in source metadata

text: On Trading Economics' disclosed CFD reference basis, Brent rose to $74.84/bbl on June 25, up 1.50% on the day but still down 22.58% over the past month and up 12.22% over 12 months. The same page said traders were monitoring Strait of Hormuz shipments after a cargo ship was struck off Oman, while other page commentary framed crude as still near pre-war levels after US-Iran peace progress improved supply expectations.

value.basis_note: Trading Economics says displayed gold prices are based on OTC/CFD instruments and do not represent official benchmark prices.
value.commentary: Trading Economics attributed the Thursday rebound to a weaker US dollar and lower Treasury yields after the PCE release.

calculation.method: vendor page reported gold level and percentage changes on an OTC/CFD reference basis; mutable vendor basis disclosed in source metadata

text: On Trading Economics' disclosed OTC/CFD reference basis, gold rose to $4,024.91 per troy ounce on June 25, up 0.63% on the day, down 10.76% over the past month and up 20.92% over 12 months. The page attributed the Thursday rebound to a weaker dollar and lower Treasury yields after the PCE release.

scheduled_catalysts: Micron fiscal Q3 earnings reaction after Wednesday after-bell release

market_session: regular US cash-equity session, fourth session after Juneteenth closure
text: The June 25, 2026 report date was a regular Thursday U.S. cash-equity and options session, the fourth session after the Juneteenth closure, so sentiment values can be read against a live U.S. closing tape and a full Cboe options settle. Two scheduled catalysts shaped the session: the 8:30 a.m. EDT May PCE inflation release and the cash-session reaction to Micron Technology's fiscal Q3 results, which had been reported after the bell on Wednesday June 24.

russell_2000.change_percent: +0.7% (+0.38% per TheStreet close update; +0.71% per AP rounded)

value.context: Mixed close: Micron-led memory rally vs Apple -6.13% and Microsoft -3.23% dragging mega-cap tech; Caterpillar +5.81% and UnitedHealth +2.65% lifting the Dow; rotation into cyclicals continued; VIX rose despite the easing in June 24's vol

text: AP reported a mixed June 25 U.S. close: the S&P 500 was essentially unchanged at 7,357.49 (-0.73 points, less than 0.1%) and the Nasdaq Composite fell 118.03 points or 0.5% to 25,358.60, its fourth straight decline, as Apple dropped about 6% after raising Mac and iPad prices and Microsoft fell 3.23% after announcing Xbox price increases. The Dow rose 71.72 points or 0.1% to 51,920.62 and the Russell 2000 gained 21.23 points or 0.7% to 3,007.86 on broader participation, with Caterpillar +5.81% and UnitedHealth +2.65% leading. The session's signal is a continuation of June 24's bifurcation: a Micron-led memory and chip rally versus persistent Magnificent 7 selling pressure, with the cap-weighted S&P 500 flat while the equal-weight and small-cap complex outperformed.

subject.name: CNN Fear & Greed Index
subject.source_basis: CNN Fear & Greed page corroborated by Finhacker.cz CNN-tracker

value.methodology_note: CNN Stock Price Breadth indicator still reflects the McClellan Summation Index methodology change noted in prior reports
value.freshness_note: CNN's 26 is an intraday June 25 snapshot (1:59 PM ET); Finhacker's 26 reflects the June 24 post-close value. Both publishers now agree on 26, unlike June 24 when CNN read 27 and Finhacker 28.

market_session: latest available readings at collection time
text: The Fear & Greed Index remained in fear territory on June 25. CNN's page displayed 26 (fear) with 'Previous close 25', '1 week ago 37', '1 month ago 58', '1 year ago 59', and 'Last updated Jun 25 at 1:59:33 PM ET'. Finhacker.cz's CNN-tracker also showed 26 (fear) with a data-updated timestamp of 'Jun 24, 2026, 8:00 PM ET'. Unlike June 24, when CNN and Finhacker disagreed by one point (27 vs 28), both publishers now converge on 26. Five of the seven CNN sub-indicators were labeled fear or extreme fear at the time of access: market momentum (fear), stock price strength (fear), stock price breadth (extreme fear, McClellan Volume Summation Index at 1,400.47), put/call options (fear, 5-day average 0.70), and market volatility (neutral, with VIX below its 50-day moving average). The composite moved up one point from the June 24 close of 25 but stayed firmly in the fear zone, down 11 points from one month ago.

nine_day_thirty_day_spread.note: VIX9D-to-VIX gap widened; the 9-day measure eased while the 30-day measure rose

market_session: Cboe published volatility-index data for the regular US session

calculation.method: VIX one-day percent change = (18.89 - 18.63) / 18.63 * 100 = +1.40%; VIX9D one-day change = (17.92 - 18.07) / 18.07 * 100 = -0.83%; curve compares Cboe close fields for VIX9D, VIX, VIX3M, VIX6M, and VIX1Y on 2026-06-25

text: Yahoo Finance historical data showed VIX closing at 18.89 on June 25, up 0.26 points or 1.40% from 18.63 on June 24, with OHLC of open 18.11, high 19.95, low 17.72, close 18.89. Investing.com corroborates at 18.90 (+1.45%) with a 4:14:46 PM close stamp. The term structure was in contango and upward sloping across all tenors: VIX9D at 17.92, VIX at 18.89, VIX3M at 20.33, VIX6M at 22.35, and VIX1Y at 23.50. The notable feature is a divergence between the 9-day and the 30-day measures: VIX9D eased 0.83% to 17.92 (the near-term event risk of the PCE morning passing without a tape break was priced out), while VIX rose 1.40% to 18.89 (the cash-session mega-cap tech selloff and the Strait of Hormuz strike repriced the 30-day window). The VIX9D-to-VIX gap widened to 0.97 points from June 24's 0.56-point spread. The longer tenors all eased slightly (VIX3M -0.20%, VIX6M -0.22%, VIX1Y -1.48%), keeping the curve in orderly contango. Front-month July VIX futures settled June 24 at 18.90, a +0.27-point premium to spot that signals mild contango rather than acute stress.

market_session: latest AAII weekly survey published for the June 25 report date

calculation.method: Bull-bear spread = bullish 44.9% minus bearish 36.1% = +8.8 percentage points; prior spread = 36.6 - 39.4 = -2.8 pp; one-week swing = 8.8 - (-2.8) = +11.6 pp

text: The latest AAII sentiment survey (week ending 6/24/2026, published Thursday June 25) showed a sharp bullish leap: bullish sentiment at 44.9%, neutral at 18.9%, and bearish at 36.1%, leaving a bull-bear spread of +8.8 percentage points. This is the first time bullish sentiment has been above its historical average of 37.5% in six weeks. The swing from the prior week's -2.8 pp spread is an 11.6-percentage-point one-week jump, the largest bullish shift since the run-up to the January 2026 highs. The AAII companion article was titled 'Optimism Leaps.' The survey period closed before the June 25 cash-session action, so the bullish print does not reflect the fourth-straight Nasdaq decline; it captures the improvement in mood driven by the Iran de-escalation, the Micron-guidance-led chip rally, and the in-line May PCE release. The special question asked about the US-Iran memorandum of understanding's impact on economic expectations.

spx_put_call_ratio.source: Barchart S&P 500 Index Put/Call for Thu Jun 25 2026

value.interpretation: Two-tier pattern: single-stock options lean bullish (more calls than puts) while index and ETF flow leans defensive (more puts than calls), consistent with institutional tail-hedging alongside retail bullishness in single names

market_session: Cboe end-of-day options statistics for the regular US session
text: Cboe end-of-day options statistics for June 25 showed a two-tier put/call pattern. The total put/call ratio was 1.02, with equity put/call at 0.61, index put/call at 1.16, and exchange-traded products put/call at 1.38. Barchart's S&P 500 index put/call for June 25 showed put volume of 907,215 against call volume of 694,491, a ratio of 1.31. The pattern - bullish single-stock options flow (equity P/C 0.61, well below 1.0) alongside defensive index and ETF hedging (index P/C 1.16, ETP P/C 1.38, SPX P/C 1.31) - is consistent with institutions buying tail protection via index products while retail positioning remains bullish on individual names, particularly the Micron-led memory and chip complex. The CNN Fear & Greed 5-day average put/call ratio of 0.70 (fear zone) is a different construction from the Cboe end-of-day ratios but points in the same direction: options-market sentiment is cautious overall, with the defensive bid concentrated in index products.

value.data_freshness_note: ICI's latest combined-flow release (published June 24 for the week ended June 17) predates the June 23-24 risk-off action and the June 25 PCE/Micron session; the next ICI release (week ended June 24) had not been published at collection time. ETF.com's June 22 weekly article captures the post-SpaceX-IPO euphoria week but also predates the late-week tech selloff. Positioning context, not real-time flow.

market_session: latest fund-flow releases available for the June 25 report date
text: The latest available fund-flow data provides positioning context heading into the June 25 session rather than a real-time flow gauge. ICI's combined-flow release (published June 24 for the week ended June 17) showed $66.23 billion of total long-term inflows, including $92.03 billion of ETF net issuance and $25.80 billion of mutual fund outflows. Equity funds took in $55.75 billion (domestic $49.60B, world $6.15B), bond funds took in $16.46 billion, hybrid funds saw $4.68 billion in outflows, and commodity funds saw $1.31 billion in outflows. ETF.com's June 22 weekly article reported $119.83 billion of total ETF inflows led by $83.62 billion into U.S. equity ETFs and $21.66 billion into international equity ETFs, with IVV (+$30.40B), SPYM (+$12.87B), XLK (+$8.33B), and SMH (+$7.44B) the top creators and SPY (-$29.62B) and VOO (-$14.42B) the top redemptions. The flows point to strong risk appetite into the late-week selloff but do not capture the June 23-25 tape action; the SPY/VOO redemptions alongside IVV/SPYM creations suggest structural rotation (share-class creation flow) rather than outright equity abandonment.

value.brent_note: reversal from pre-war drift on Strait of Hormuz vessel strike

may_headline_pce.note: three-year high, in line with forecasts

value.macro_theme: Iran de-escalation vs Hormuz flare-up, PCE at three-year high but in line, Micron-led risk-on in chips, mega-cap tech drag

text: Reuters reported Brent crude settled up 2% at $75.26 a barrel, reversing several days of declines toward pre-US-Iran-war levels on reports of an Iranian strike on a vessel in the Strait of Hormuz. The dollar index eased 0.15% to 101.45 from Wednesday's one-year high. Gold recovered from a sub-$4,000 print to around $4,045. The 10-year Treasury yield slipped 0.59 bp to 4.394%, its lowest since mid-May, as the May PCE report came in at a three-year-high 4.1% year-over-year headline (3.4% core) but matched forecasts. The macro backdrop is contradictory for sentiment: the in-line PCE print and easing yields are risk-positive, the Hormuz strike and the 4%+ inflation are risk-negative, and the cross-asset price action - Brent up, dollar down, gold up, yields down - is a classic geopolitical-risk bid rather than a clean risk-on or risk-off tape. Against this backdrop, the AAII survey's jump to net bullish reflects the Iran de-escalation narrative and the chip rally more than it prices the lingering mega-cap tech overhang.

subject.label: rotational risk-on with institutional hedging, mega-cap tech still bleeding

value.cash_equity_tape: mixed close with S&P flat, Nasdaq -0.5% (4th straight), Dow +0.1%, Russell +0.7%; Caterpillar +5.81%, UnitedHealth +2.65%, Micron +15% offset Apple -6.13%, Microsoft -3.23%
value.survey_sentiment: sharp AAII bullish leap to 44.9% / +8.8 pp spread, first above-average in 6 weeks
value.volatility: VIX +1.40% to 18.89; VIX9D -0.83% to 17.92; contango curve with widening 9D-30D gap
value.options: two-tier: equity P/C 0.61 (bullish single names), index P/C 1.16 and ETP P/C 1.38 (defensive hedging)
value.flows: latest available flows are pre-late-week-selloff; $83B into US equity ETFs in the SpaceX-IPO week
value.fear_greed: CNN and Finhacker converge at 26 (fear), down from 37 a week ago
value.risk_read: sentiment is off the June 23 panic lows but not risk-on; the bullish narrative is driven by chips and Iran de-escalation, while mega-cap tech weakness and PCE above 4% cap the upside

market_session: regular US cash-equity session synthesis from latest available sentiment indicators
text: The best synthesis for June 25 is 'rotational risk-on under tail hedge, mega-cap tech still bleeding.' The AAII survey's 11.6-percentage-point one-week swing to a +8.8 pp bull-bear spread is the clearest sentiment signal of the cycle, and it aligns with the cash-session action: money flowed into the Micron-led memory and chip complex (+15% on Micron, +22% on Sandisk), into cyclicals (Caterpillar +5.81%), and into small caps (Russell +0.7%), while the Magnificent 7 continued to bleed (Apple -6.13%, Microsoft -3.23%) and the Nasdaq booked a fourth straight decline. The Fear & Greed Index held at 26 (fear) with CNN and Finhacker now converging, and the CNN sub-indicators showed extreme fear in stock price breadth (McClellan Volume Summation Index at 1,400.47) despite the neutral reading on market volatility. The VIX term structure captured the cross-currents perfectly: VIX9D eased 0.83% as the PCE event cleared, while VIX rose 1.40% as the cash-session tech selloff and the Hormuz strike repriced the 30-day window, widening the 9D-to-30D gap to 0.97 points. The Cboe put/call pattern - equity 0.61, index 1.16, ETP 1.38 - confirms that institutions are paying up for tail protection via index products while staying long the single-stock stories they like. The regime is therefore not the June 23 unambiguous risk-off and not a clean risk-on: it is a rotational, narrative-driven risk appetite (chips, Iran de-escalation, in-line PCE) layered over a persistent mega-cap tech overhang and an unresolved above-4% inflation backdrop, with institutional hedging visibly bid.
